title: Exception to the Exception: A Win for the Netherlands Institute of Sound and Vision
content:
Blog post by Maarten Zeinstra

In November 2025, Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) reported on the systemic challenges facing the Out-of-Commerce (OOC) regime under the EU Copyright in the Digital Single Market (DSM) Directive. KR21, COMMUNIA, and Europeana all argue that significant structural flaws within Articles 8–11 heavily restrict the efficacy of the regime.

However, a recent Dutch success story serves as a notable exception to the slow implementation and systemic bottlenecks seen across Europe. This larger publication of out-of-commerce heritage is the result of a “perfect storm” of policy, funding, and proactive politics. It points to what is possible.

In May 2026, the Netherlands Institute of Sound and Vision (Beeld & Geluid) launched a new collection portal called De Schatkamer (The Treasury). The platform unlocks over 700,000 audio and audiovisual archival items that were previously unavailable on the open internet. These include daily historical newsreels from Dutch public broadcasters and iconic television series—such as Floris starring Rutger Hauer—that are no longer available through customary commercial channels.
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: Because, Because … ! European Commission Says Consumers Take a Back Seat to Copyright: Erasure of Content Can Continue
content:
Stop Killing Games is just the 14th European Citizens’ Initiative (ECI) in as many years to have received the over 1 million signatures necessary to trigger an obligation for the European Commission to provide a response.

Unfortunately, the response is a limp one, which can be summarised as ‘because because’, or in more words, ‘we can’t change the law because we can’t change the law’. For an institution that is all about making and changing laws, this is curious.

The Commission’s response does, however, increase the pressure to look again at copyright laws themselves in order to ensure that they support the EU’s wider consumer protection goals. In this, the gaming community will find strong allies in libraries, universities, and similar institutions.
snippet: Stop Killing Games is just the 14th European Citizens’ Initiative (ECI) in as many years to have received the over 1 million signatures necessary to trigger an obligation for the European Commission
creator: Stephen Wyber
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: Europe’s Copyright Framework Is Failing Researchers
content:
Blog Post by Dr Konrad Gliściński LL.M. (Intellectual Property Law Chair JU/ Centrum Cyfrowe)

Research today is international, digital, data-intensive, and collaborative by default. Scientists routinely work across borders, rely on access to large collections of publications and data, use computational methods such as text and data mining (TDM), and increasingly incorporate AI tools into their research workflows. Yet the legal framework governing access to and use of copyrighted materials for research remains fragmented along national lines.

A new report, Barriers to Research: Insights into the Patchwork of Research Exceptions in European Copyright Laws and Practical Perspectives from European Researchers, prepared by Centrum Cyfrowe in collaboration with the Future Law Lab of the Jagiellonian University, shows that despite decades of copyright harmonisation efforts at the EU level, researchers across Europe still operate within a highly fragmented legal framework, resulting in legal uncertainty and unnecessary obstacles.
snippet: Blog Post by Dr Konrad Gliściński LL.M. (Intellectual Property Law Chair JU/ Centrum Cyfrowe)
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: It Just Ain’t Right: Why Open Access Mandates Without Secondary Publication Rights Are a Recipe for Unfairness
content:
It is looking increasingly likely that a secondary publication right (SPR) will feature on the European Union’s legislative agenda in the coming years. Having been clearly called for by Member States in Council Conclusions in 2023, the European Commission has commissioned two significant pieces of research into SPRs and their impacts. 

Central to the debate ahead is a distinction that often goes unstated: the difference between an obligation to publish open access and a right that makes doing so legally safe for researchers.

Furthermore, it has now called twice for stakeholder inputs, firstly in a consultation led by the Directorate General for Research, Technology and Innovation (DG RTD) in the second half of 2025, and now in one led by the Directorate General for Communications Networks, Content and Technology (DG CNECT), which is open until 25 June 2026.
snippet: It is looking increasingly likely that a secondary publication right (SPR) will feature on the European Union’s legislative agenda in the coming years. Having been clearly called for by Member States
creator: Stephen Wyber

title: Rome Conference Charts the Road Ahead for Open Science under the European Research Area Act
content:
Blog post by Deborah De Angelis

On 10 June 2026, the conference “Open Science through the Prism of the European Research Area (ERA) Act: The Work Still to Be Done” took place at the Central Library of the National Research Council (CNR) in Rome. The event provided an important opportunity for discussion, bringing together policymakers, scholars, legal experts, researchers, institutional representatives, and professionals to address a key issue for the future of European research.

Institutional Voices Set the Tone & Political Commitment for Open Science and SPR
snippet: Blog post by Deborah De Angelis
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: No Future? Act Now to Save Tomorrow’s Yesterday
content:
Last week marked International Archives Week (8–12 June 2026), leaving behind a question worth lingering on:  why do we remember?

Archives, alongside libraries and other heritage institutions, are essential to ensuring that there is someone to take on the (commercially) thankless task of collecting, organising, and providing access to our history. This is why they are legislated for, given legal mandates and funding, in order to fulfil a role that no-one else will.

As the International Council on Archives points out, this activity is not a nice-to-have, but rather essential to delivering on the rule of law, protecting rights, ensuring accountability, enabling memory and dialogue, and addressing injustice.

At a time of rising populism, dramatic political changes, and the risks that this brings of infringements of rights, the need to archive the present is as great as ever.
snippet: Last week marked International Archives Week (8–12 June 2026), leaving behind a question worth lingering on: why do we remember?
creator: Stephen Wyber
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: European Rights Retention Community of Practice – Tenth Meeting
content:
When?

  • Date: 9 July 2026
  • Time: 12:00 – 13:30 CEST

    • What?

    This Community of Practice is an online collaborative space designed to bring together professionals to discuss and tackle the challenges around rights retention strategies.

    Save Your Spot!

    Register now to secure your spot.

    The Webinar

    This 10th meeting of the community, organised by Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) partner SPARC Europe, explores how open science and rights retention support academic freedom, drawing on the expertise of:

  • Ana Lazarova | Digital Republic – KR21 national coordinator, Bulgaria
  • Lionel Maurel | CNRS, France
  • Sam Moore | University of Cambridge, UK

    • Following the presentations, community members will have the opportunity to share their own experiences, challenges, and insights in an open discussion.

    Why You Should Attend

    Joining this session is an opportunity to continue bringing together experiences on Rights Retention from across Europe to inspire and strengthen our community.

    9 June 2026

    The post European Rights Retention Community of Practice – Tenth Meeting appeared first on Knowledge Rights 21.

title: The Digital Memory We Cannot Afford to Lose: Knowledge Rights 21 Signs the Our Future Memory Statement
content:
Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) has signed the Our Future Memory Statement on the Four Digital Rights of Memory Institutions. We join the International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions (IFLA), the International Council on Archives (ICA), the Wikimedia Foundation, and hundreds of libraries, archives, and museums across six continents in calling on policymakers to ensure that memory institutions retain online the same rights they have always held offline: the right to collect, to preserve, to provide access, and to cooperate across borders.

The timing is not coincidental. The urgency is real.

In May 2026, Mark Graham, the director of the Wayback Machine, described it as “collateral damage caught up in the conflict between AI companies and publishers.” News publishers, including some of the world’s most prominent titles, have begun blocking the Internet Archive from archiving their websites. The stated reason is concerns about AI scraping. The effect is that the public record of those publications, part of our collective memory, is no longer being preserved.
snippet: Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) has signed the Our Future Memory Statement on the Four Digital Rights of Memory Institutions. We join the International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions
creator: KR21
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: Sofia University Library Awarded “Library of the Year 2025” in Bulgaria for Leadership in Open Science
content:
Blog post by Ana Lazarova

On May 14, 2026, the Sofia University Library “St. Kliment Ohridski” – the academic library of Bulgaria’s oldest and largest university – received Bulgaria’s Library of the Year 2025 award. The distinction was presented at the XVI Annual Library Awards ceremony, organised by the Bulgarian Library and Information Association during the XX National Library Week. The University library was nominated by the Academic Council of Sofia University in recognition of its achievements in open science and digitisation.

The main reason for the nomination and the award was the library’s active implementation of the secondary publication mechanisms adopted at the national level in recent years.

In late 2023, Bulgaria adopted a Secondary Publishing Right (SPR) into its copyright legislation – Article 60, paragraphs 2 and following of the Copyright and Neighbouring Rights Act. This created an imperative regime preventing publishers from overriding specific author reuse rights.
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: National Workshop on Public Lending Right: Strengthening Moldova’s Path Toward EU Integration
content:
Blog post by Mariana Harjevschi

On 23 and 24  April 2026, coinciding with World Book and Copyright Day, the National Workshop on Public Lending Right (PLR) took place in Chișinău in a hybrid format. The event brought together policymakers, international experts, and representatives of libraries, authors’ organisations, and cultural institutions to advance discussions on introducing Public Lending Right in the Republic of Moldova.

The workshop was organised by the State Agency on Intellectual Property (AGEPI) with the support of the EU4IP project and Swiss Partnership Moldova. It formed part of the broader national effort to align copyright legislation with the EU acquis and to explore sustainable policy options for implementing PLR.

The opening session featured contributions from institutional and international partners, highlighting the importance of coordinated action between government bodies, libraries, and the cultural sector. Discussions underscored that the implementation of PLR requires not only legal adjustments but also institutional readiness and sustainable financing mechanisms.
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!
